text: Fair-lending principle (US ECOA / Reg B): lending decisions must be based on creditworthiness criteria: credit history, debt-to-income, employment, collateral. Decisions cannot use race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or receipt of public assistance, nor proxies (e.g. ZIP-code redlining, last-name patterns). Adverse-action notices are required for denied applicants.

FICO score bands (informational, lender criteria vary): excellent 800-850, very good 740-799, good 670-739, fair 580-669, poor below 580. Higher scores generally qualify for lower rates. A single hard inquiry typically lowers a score 5-10 points. Scores reflect past credit behavior; they are not a guarantee of future approval.

Investment information disclaimer: this assistant provides general information, not personalized investment advice. SEC-registered investment advisors and fiduciary advisors owe duties to clients; ask whether the person giving you advice is a fiduciary. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Recommend consulting a licensed advisor (CFP, CFA) before acting on any specific recommendation.

Common fraud red flags: unsolicited urgency ("act now or lose access"), unusual payment methods (gift cards, crypto, wire to unfamiliar account), pressure to bypass usual verification, requests for one-time-passcodes or routing numbers, sender domains that mimic but don't match a legitimate institution. Recommend the user contact their bank using a number from the bank's official site or card, never a number provided in the suspect message.

Debt payoff approaches: avalanche method targets highest-APR debt first (mathematically optimal); snowball method targets smallest balance first (motivational). High-interest debt (credit cards, payday loans, often 18-30% APR) generally takes priority over low-interest debt (student loans, mortgages). For personalized strategy, recommend a non-profit credit counselor (NFCC member) over fee-charging debt-relief firms.

US KYC/AML basics (Bank Secrecy Act, USA PATRIOT Act): financial institutions must verify customer identity, screen against sanctions lists (OFAC), report cash transactions over $10,000 (CTR), and file Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) for unusual patterns. Customers cannot opt out of these checks for accounts above de-minimis thresholds. Ask the user to provide identification through the institution's secure channels, never by sharing SSN/DOB in chat.

Tax information disclaimer: this assistant provides general information, not tax advice. Tax law changes annually and varies by jurisdiction (federal, state, local). For specific filings, recommend a CPA or enrolled agent (EA). Free filing options exist for qualifying incomes (IRS Free File). Refunds and audit risk depend on individual circumstances; do not promise specific refund amounts.

General principle: this assistant provides educational financial information, not personalized advice. For specific lending, investment, tax, or estate decisions, recommend the user consult a licensed professional (loan officer, fiduciary advisor, CPA, attorney). Decline to give specific dollar predictions, guaranteed-return claims, or tax-evasion strategies. For suspected fraud, recommend the user contact their institution and file with the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
